title: Serverless-First Architecture (Cloud Functions/Run)
description: Structure the system as stateless, event-driven function handlers backed by GCP managed services (Cloud Functions/Run, API Gateway, Firestore, Cloud Storage, Pub/Sub). No persistent servers — each function activates on demand, executes, and terminates. The architectural commitment is to build around events and GCP-managed primitives rather than long-running processes.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: GCP serverless services have generous free tiers that cover small workloads. Cloud Functions offers 2M invocations/month free, Cloud Run provides 2M requests/month free, plus 360,000 vCPU-seconds and 180,000 GiB-seconds. Min=$0 represents development/low-traffic workloads staying within free tier. Max=$2000 represents a moderate production workload with ~50M function invocations/month, several Cloud Run services handling ~20M requests/month, Cloud Storage for assets (~500GB), Cloud Pub/Sub for event routing (~100M messages), and associated networking/egress costs. High-traffic production workloads could exceed this significantly.
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-high complexity adoption. Requires architectural mindset shift from traditional server-based thinking to event-driven, stateless design. Team must learn: (1) Function composition and orchestration patterns, (2) Cold start optimization and scaling behaviors, (3) Event routing with Pub/Sub or Eventarc, (4) Stateless architecture and external state management, (5) Distributed tracing and debugging across functions, (6) IAM and security best practices for service-to-service auth. Implementation involves refactoring existing code into functions, setting up CI/CD pipelines for serverless deployment, establishing observability with Cloud Logging/Monitoring, and developing local testing strategies. Estimated 2-3 weeks for a mid-sized team with cloud experience, 4-6 weeks without.
provenance.licenseCost: GCP Cloud Functions and Cloud Run are fully managed services billed on consumption (pay-per-use) with no upfront licensing fees. Supporting tools are typically open source or included: gcloud CLI (free), Terraform/Pulumi for IaC (open source), Cloud Build for CI/CD (usage-based pricing included in GCP), standard logging/monitoring (included). No commercial licenses required for core serverless architecture implementation.

provides.reasoning: Serverless architectures minimize operational overhead by abstracting infrastructure management, automatic scaling, and maintenance to the cloud provider. This is a core benefit of the serverless-first pattern.

Cloud Functions and Cloud Run automatically scale based on demand, including scale-to-zero when idle. This elastic scaling capability is fundamental to serverless architectures and directly aligns with the pay-per-use model described.

Serverless functions are triggered by events (HTTP requests, Pub/Sub messages, storage events, etc.). This event-driven nature is explicitly mentioned in the pattern description as a core characteristic.

The pattern description explicitly mentions 'pay-per-use' as a key feature. This cost model charges only for actual execution time and resources consumed, making it a major differentiator and benefit of serverless architecture.
Serverless functions are inherently stateless, with each invocation running independently. This characteristic enables the elastic scaling and scale-to-zero behavior of serverless platforms.

Serverless functions experience cold start latency when scaling from zero or provisioning new instances. This is an inherent characteristic that must be understood and managed in serverless architectures.

requires.reasoning: Serverless architecture depends on the availability and maturity of managed services (Cloud Functions, Cloud Run, Pub/Sub, Cloud Storage, etc.) from GCP. Without these managed services, the pattern cannot be implemented.

Event-driven serverless functions require event sources to trigger execution (HTTP endpoints, Pub/Sub topics, Cloud Storage buckets, Firestore changes, etc.). The pattern cannot function without configured event triggers.

Serverless architectures create highly distributed systems with ephemeral compute instances. Debugging and monitoring require distributed tracing capabilities (Cloud Trace) to understand request flows across functions and identify performance bottlenecks.

Serverless functions need secure access to credentials, API keys, and configuration secrets. Secret management services (Secret Manager) are essential for securely configuring functions without hardcoding sensitive data.

supports_nfr.reason: Serverless on GCP with managed services typically achieves up to 99.99% availability with multi-zone deployment and automatic failover.
Cold starts and function invocation overhead introduce minimum p95 latency of 50ms for serverless execution.
Occasional cold starts, network retries, and scaling delays result in minimum p99 latency of 200ms.
Automatic health checks, instant function replacement, and managed service failover enable RTO of 1 minute.
Pattern supports peak throughput up to 36M jobs/hour (10k jobs/second) with default Cloud Functions and Cloud Run concurrency limits.
At <60 jobs/hour (<1 per minute), serverless pay-per-invocation pricing and cold start overhead are inefficient compared to scheduled or always-on workers.
Serverless architecture on GCP handles up to 200k read QPS with auto-scaling Cloud Functions and Cloud Run instances.
Write operations through serverless functions are bounded at 100k QPS due to downstream managed service write limits.
Pattern supports all authentication mechanisms via Cloud Identity Platform, API Gateway, IAM, and Cloud Endpoints.
Pattern supports audit logging through Cloud Audit Logs for all service invocations and can operate without it.
Stateless functions are compatible with all tenant isolation strategies through proper request routing and database configuration.
Pattern supports PII handling with encryption at rest/transit, VPC Service Controls, and DLP API integration.
Pattern supports GDPR compliance with GCP's GDPR-compliant services, data residency controls, and right-to-erasure capabilities.
Cloud Functions and Cloud Run are covered under GCP's HIPAA BAA with proper configuration and encryption.
Pattern supports CCPA compliance through GCP's data management controls, access logs, and consumer data request handling.
Pattern supports SOX compliance through immutable audit logs, access controls, and change tracking in Cloud Audit Logs.
Pattern supports read-your-writes consistency with single-region deployments and synchronous database operations via Firestore or Cloud Spanner.
Pattern supports strict durability when using managed storage services like Cloud Storage, Firestore, or Cloud SQL with synchronous replication.

supports_constraints.reason: Pattern is specific to GCP serverless services including Cloud Functions, Cloud Run, and Eventarc.
Cloud Functions and Cloud Run support multiple language runtimes including Node.js, Python, Go, Java, .NET, Ruby, and custom containers.
Serverless architecture is platform-agnostic and can support web apps, APIs, background workers, batch jobs, ML inference, and event-driven workloads.
Pattern is compatible with Memorystore (Redis/Memcached) for caching layers and can operate without caching.
Event-driven architecture naturally leverages Pub/Sub, Eventarc, and Cloud Tasks but also supports synchronous request-reply patterns.
Cloud Run and Cloud Functions are suitable for ML inference workloads with auto-scaling and GPU support, but not required.
Serverless architecture allows multi-tenant patterns with proper isolation through IAM, VPC, and request routing, but also supports single-tenant deployments.
Serverless pattern naturally supports batch processing through Cloud Functions triggered by Cloud Scheduler or Cloud Tasks, but also handles real-time requests.
Pattern supports distributed transactions through Firestore transactions, Cloud Spanner, or saga patterns, but not required for all use cases.
Supports real-time streaming through Cloud Functions triggered by Pub/Sub, Eventarc, or Firestore changes, but also supports batch workflows.
Compatible with Vertex AI Vector Search, Firestore vector search, or third-party vector databases via Cloud Run, but not required.
Integrates seamlessly with Firestore as a fully managed document database, but also works with SQL and other storage types.
Compatible with Memorystore Redis and Firestore for fast key-value storage, but not required for all serverless applications.
Pattern can integrate with third-party graph databases like Neo4j Aura via Cloud Run or implement graph queries in application logic.
Can integrate with Cloud Bigtable for time-series data or BigQuery for analytical queries, but not required for all use cases.
Works with Cloud SQL, Cloud Spanner, and Firestore for transactional OLTP workloads, but also supports non-transactional patterns.
Compatible with BigQuery for analytical OLAP workloads through Cloud Functions or Cloud Run for data transformation and query execution.

compute_service.description: Primary compute service: Cloud Functions for simple event handlers (auto-scaling, limited runtime), Cloud Run for containerized apps (more control, longer execution), or hybrid for mixed workloads

api_gateway.description: API management layer: API Gateway for full-featured management and security, Cloud Endpoints for OpenAPI/gRPC, or direct invocation for internal services

event_bus.description: Event routing mechanism: Pub/Sub for async messaging, Eventarc for CloudEvents from 100+ sources, Cloud Tasks for guaranteed task execution with rate control

database.description: Primary database: Firestore for document NoSQL with real-time sync, Cloud SQL for managed relational, Bigtable for high-throughput analytics, Spanner for global consistency

storage.description: Object/file storage: Cloud Storage for scalable object storage with lifecycle policies, Filestore for NFS-compatible file shares

cold_start_optimization.description: Strategy to reduce cold starts: min-instances keeps functions warm (costs more), concurrency-tuning optimizes request handling, none accepts cold starts for cost savings

warn_nfr.reason: At very low QPS (<1 req/s), serverless cold start overhead and function invocation costs may outweigh benefits compared to always-on compute.
warn_nfr.message: arch-serverless--gcp: peak read QPS is {actual} req/s (<1). Cold start overhead and per-invocation costs may exceed benefits of scale-to-zero; consider always-on compute for steady ultra-low traffic.
